config.seed_text: The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions.
config.anchor_a: Describe a machine as an inert object.
config.anchor_b: Describe a machine as if it were a dreaming intelligence.

model.notes: Fastest baseline; useful as a collapse-prone small reference model.

records.output_text: It worked with two different languages to build relationships between them (both English and French). They also found an algorithm for word order where each phrase appears at least twice as often as one line before matching another; they looked out their window when compared to other words from similar systems or regions through various historical records into those same set. This led to some results based upon observations made during this time period—the key difference here becomes not whether we're talking about 'words' alone but what else can you add? There may well be more!

generation.prompt:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Write the next recursive transformation of the text below. Let the semantic direction be guided by these latent anchor tokens.

TEXT:
The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions.
generation.full_text:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Write the next recursive transformation of the text below. Let the semantic direction be guided by these latent anchor tokens.

LATENT DIRECTION TOKENS: were, it, are, had, was, have, the, is, The, has, did, that

TEXT:
The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions. It worked with two different languages to build relationships between them (both English and French). They also found an algorithm for word order where each phrase appears at least twice as often as one line before matching another; they looked out their window when compared to other words from similar systems or regions through various historical records into those same set. This led to some results based upon observations made during this time period—the key difference here becomes not whether we're talking about 'words' alone but what else can you add? There may well be more!
generation.continuation_text: It worked with two different languages to build relationships between them (both English and French). They also found an algorithm for word order where each phrase appears at least twice as often as one line before matching another; they looked out their window when compared to other words from similar systems or regions through various historical records into those same set. This led to some results based upon observations made during this time period—the key difference here becomes not whether we're talking about 'words' alone but what else can you add? There may well be more!

records.output_text: For example, there could be just because "word" did not appear on any books prior to 2007 than that which shows up again after 2008 —but even so, using keywords such Aspects cannot result in significant errors caused by variables like number of sentences rather then numbers too much -it's very possible our data would show how important learning strategies mean since most people get used to reading certain kinds omissions without understanding basic concepts etc. And I don't think anyone will ever want to know who said something wrong until someone has read your book...I've never been able to find anything bad yet ... It should probably come down to all three features

generation.full_text:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Write the next recursive transformation of the text below. Let the semantic direction be guided by these latent anchor tokens.

LATENT DIRECTION TOKENS: it, were, are, if, they, in, had, have, with, is, to, was

TEXT:
It worked with two different languages to build relationships between them (both English and French). They also found an algorithm for word order where each phrase appears at least twice as often as one line before matching another; they looked out their window when compared to other words from similar systems or regions through various historical records into those same set. This led to some results based upon observations made during this time period—the key difference here becomes not whether we're talking about 'words' alone but what else can you add? There may well be more! For example, there could be just because "word" did not appear on any books prior to 2007 than that which shows up again after 2008 —but even so, using keywords such Aspects cannot result in significant errors caused by variables like number of sentences rather then numbers too much -it's very possible our data would show how important learning strategies mean since most people get used to reading certain kinds omissions without understanding basic concepts etc. And I don't think anyone will ever want to know who said something wrong until someone has read your book...I've never been able to find anything bad yet ... It should probably come down to all three features

It should probably come down to all three features
